confidence.disclosure: Umar's caliphate (634-644) is one of the best-documented periods of early Islamic history. His single major journey — north to Jabiya and Jerusalem in 637 — is attested in both Muslim and Christian sources. His administrative life in Medina is well recorded. The main uncertainty is his pre-conversion period in Mecca, where traditional reports of his hostility to Islam may have been embellished by later writers to dramatise his conversion.

journey.event: Born in Mecca into the Banu Adi clan of the Quraysh tribe, about thirteen years after the Prophet Muhammad. His father al-Khattab ibn Nufayl was known for his harsh temperament and insisted on a strict upbringing.

Grew up in Mecca learning to read and write — a rare skill among the Quraysh — and became a capable merchant and renowned wrestler. He was also known as an arbitrator of disputes among the clans of Mecca before the rise of Islam.

Converted to Islam after a period of fierce hostility toward the Muslim community. According to tradition, he set out intending to kill the Prophet but was moved to tears upon hearing verses of Surah Ta-Ha recited at his sister Fatimah's house, and immediately went to the Prophet to declare his faith.

Immediately after converting, Umar insisted on praying openly at the Kaaba, emboldening the Muslim community which had been worshipping in secret. His physical strength and tribal standing gave the small community a new sense of protection in Mecca.

Endured persecution from the Quraysh after his conversion, including hostility from members of his own Banu Adi clan. Despite his formidable reputation, he was no longer afforded the full protection of his kinsmen and faced social isolation.

Emigrated from Mecca to Medina as part of the Hijra, reportedly one of the few Muslims who migrated openly rather than in secret. According to tradition, he announced his departure at the Kaaba and challenged anyone to stop him, and none dared.

Fought at the Battle of Badr, the first major military engagement between the Muslims and the Quraysh of Mecca. The Muslim force of roughly 313 men defeated a Quraysh army nearly three times their number, a victory seen as divine vindication of the new community.

Fought at the Battle of Uhud where the Muslims suffered a setback after archers abandoned their positions. Umar stood firm around the Prophet when many fled, receiving wounds in the fighting and helping to rally the remaining defenders on the mountainside.

Participated in the Battle of the Trench, where a confederation of Quraysh and allied tribes besieged Medina. Umar helped dig the defensive trench around the city on the advice of Salman al-Farisi and took part in the weeks-long standoff until the coalition withdrew.

Present at the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, where the Prophet negotiated a ten-year truce with the Quraysh. Umar initially opposed the terms as humiliating to the Muslims and confronted Abu Bakr about them, though he later acknowledged the wisdom of the agreement.

Participated in the conquest of the fortified Jewish settlement of Khaybar, north of Medina. The campaign secured the northern approaches to Medina and established a model for the treatment of conquered agricultural communities through revenue-sharing arrangements.

Entered Mecca with the Prophet during the peaceful conquest of the city, which fell with minimal bloodshed after the Quraysh broke the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. The idols around the Kaaba were destroyed and a general amnesty was declared for the people of Mecca.

Fought at the Battle of Hunayn shortly after the conquest of Mecca, where a large Muslim force was ambushed by the Hawazin tribe in a narrow valley. After initial confusion and retreat, the Muslims rallied around the Prophet and won a decisive victory.

Marched with the Prophet on the expedition to Tabuk near the Byzantine frontier, the largest and most distant campaign of the Prophet's lifetime. Though no major battle occurred, the expedition demonstrated Muslim military reach to the northern Arabian borders and secured the submission of several border communities.

Accompanied the Prophet on the Farewell Pilgrimage, the only Hajj the Prophet performed. At the plain of Arafat, the Prophet delivered his famous sermon establishing principles of equality, justice, and the sanctity of life that would guide the community after his death.

Became the second Rashidun caliph following the death of Abu Bakr, who had nominated him as successor on his deathbed. Umar took the title Amir al-Mu'minin (Commander of the Faithful), the first ruler in Islam to bear that title, and immediately began organizing the rapidly expanding state.

Ordered the continuation and expansion of military campaigns into Syria and Iraq, dispatching commanders including Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah and Khalid ibn al-Walid. His strategic direction from Medina coordinated multiple fronts against the Byzantine and Sasanian empires simultaneously.

Directed the decisive Battle of Yarmouk from Medina, where Muslim forces under Abu Ubayda and Khalid ibn al-Walid routed the Byzantine army near the Yarmouk River. This victory effectively ended Byzantine control of the Levant and opened the way for the Muslim conquest of Syria and Palestine.

Travelled north from Medina to Jabiya in the Golan Heights, his only major journey outside the Hijaz as caliph. He received the formal surrender of the Damascus region and met with his commanders to organize the administration of the newly conquered Syrian territories.

Received the keys of Jerusalem from Patriarch Sophronius and issued the Covenant of Umar, guaranteeing the safety of the city's Christian inhabitants, their churches, and their property. He prayed at the Temple Mount and ordered the clearing of the site, which had been used as a rubbish dump under Byzantine rule.

Founded Kufa as a garrison city (misr) in Iraq to house Muslim soldiers and serve as a base for further campaigns eastward. The city was strategically placed near the ruins of the Sasanian capital at Ctesiphon and would grow into one of the most important centres of Islamic learning and politics.

Founded Basra as a second garrison city in southern Iraq, positioned to control the head of the Persian Gulf and the routes into the Iranian plateau. Along with Kufa, Basra became a key node in the administrative and military infrastructure of the expanding caliphate.

Established the Islamic Hijri calendar, designating the Prophet's emigration from Mecca to Medina in 622 CE as Year One. The decision was taken in consultation with Ali ibn Abi Talib and other senior companions to standardize dating across the growing empire's correspondence and records.

Directed the conquest of Egypt from Medina, dispatching Amr ibn al-As with a small force that eventually took the Byzantine fortress of Babylon and then Alexandria. The fall of Egypt gave the caliphate control of the Mediterranean's richest grain-producing province and opened the path to North Africa.

Assassinated by Abu Lu'lu'a Firuz, a Persian slave of al-Mughira ibn Shu'ba, who stabbed him six times with a double-bladed dagger while he led the Fajr prayer. Umar died three days later and was buried beside the Prophet Muhammad and Abu Bakr in the chamber of Aisha.
